1:
The chapter opens the roll of David's conquests, and the witnesses read its first verse both as history and as a window onto the chronicler's method. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the opening formula, "Now after this it came to pass," which he renders literally "And it befell afterwards" — a phrase that does not fix these events in strict chronological sequence with the preceding chapter, but is merely a mark of transition, the formula of the older history taken over unaltered after the manner of Oriental compilers. He then unfolds the crux of the verse. Where Chronicles says David took "Gath and her towns" — literally "her daughters," the outlying dependencies — Samuel reads that David took "the bridle of the mother-city" out of the hand of the Philistines. The chronicler, Ellicott judges, has interpreted that obscure expression: if Gath was then the chief city of Philistia and David made it own his suzerainty by tribute, both books say the same thing, the "bridle of the arm" meaning sovereign control or supremacy. He notes that in Solomon's day Gath was still ruled by a king, Achish, though hardly independent of Solomon.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same grammatical-historical school, seize on precisely this as evidence of the chronicler's independence. The figurative "bridle of the mother" is here explained by the plain "Gath and her cities"; and this paraphrastic habit — with the parallel case at v.17, where the priestly word is rendered "the first at the king's hand" — proves, they argue, that the author of Chronicles did not simply copy the second book of Samuel but drew on the common older source, adding notes peculiar to himself.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the verse from philology to praise. After the sweet communion David had enjoyed with God in the previous chapter's word and prayer, he went on his work with extraordinary vigour, "conquering and to conquer," as Jacob after his vision "lifted up his feet" in Gen. 29:1. And he draws the first of his standing lessons: those long enemies to the Israel of God are brought down at last — the Philistines, vexatious for generations, are now subdued, a pledge that all opposing rule and power shall at the end of time be put down by the Son of David, before whom the most inveterate enemies must fall. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the map of the whole campaign the chapter will trace — Gath, Moab, Hadarezer of Zobah, the Syrians of Damascus, the tribute of Tou, the dedicated spoil, the rout of Edom — and refers the reader throughout to the parallel account of 2 Samuel 8. The witnesses converge: the same events, one narrator's fresh hand, and a king whose victories are the visible seal of the covenant made in the chapter before.
2:
With Moab reduced to tribute, the two grammatical-historical witnesses turn to a conspicuous silence in the chronicler's account and ask what it means. Keil and Delitzsch note that, compared with the parallel, the notice is much abridged; and the omission that surprises them is the chronicler's passing over David's harsh treatment of the Moabite prisoners — the measuring-line and the partial massacre that Samuel records after the words "he smote Moab." They will not let a common apologetic explain it away. The claim that the chronicler suppressed the episode to shield David from the charge of barbarity is, they say, disposed of by the plain fact that he does not spare the reader the equally cruel treatment of the conquered at Rabbah in 1 Chronicles 20:3. Rather, the abridgment belongs to the chronicler's whole method: he has historical notes peculiar to himself, wanting in Samuel, which prove that he did not derive his account from the second book of Samuel but drew independently on the older source.

Charles Ellicott reaches the same verdict from the side of temper and idiom. The omission, he agrees, is scarcely due to any unfair bias; as a Jew filled with his nation's hatred of the aliens who had so often oppressed his people, the chronicler was hardly likely to regard the slaughter of captive Moabites from a modern point of view — and he has, after all, related the cruel handling of the Ammonite prisoners in the same book. Ellicott then fixes the grammar. "And the Moabites became" is literally "and they became," the singular verb showing that the name of the country stands for its people; "David's servants" answers to Samuel's "to David for servants"; and "brought gifts" is literally "bringers of an offering," that is, tribute. Such notices of subject peoples rendering tribute, he observes, are common in the Assyrian inscriptions, and he points beyond the text to the famous Moabite Stone, whose fragments record Mesha's later revolt against the house of Ahab — the same Moab, generations on, throwing off the yoke that David here lays upon it, in the pattern foreseen by the oracle of Numbers 24:17.

The witnesses do not divide. Both read the verse as a specimen of how Chronicles handles its material — compressing, yet not whitewashing — and both find in David's subjugation of Moab the sober fulfilment of an old word: a border secured, a tributary bound, and the darker facts of conquest neither denied nor dwelt upon. What the parallel tells at length the chronicler gathers into a phrase, and the witnesses agree that the compression is a matter of method, not of shame.
3:
Here the campaign moves north against the strongest of David's foes, and the name of that foe is itself the first problem. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that Chronicles writes "Hadarezer" where Samuel has "Hadadezer" — and it is Samuel's form, he insists, that is correct, for it means "Hadad is help," Hadad being a Syrian god, identical with Dadda or Rimmon, worshipped from the Euphrates to Edom and North Arabia. He supports the reading from the royal names built on the same divine element, Benhadad and Abdadad, "servant of Hadad" — formed, he notes, like Obadiah, "servant of Iah." So the chronicler's "Hadarezer" is a slip of the letters, and the god's name lies buried in the king's.

Ellicott then sets the geography. Zobah lay "towards Hamath," somewhere near Emesa — the modern Homs — not far from Yabrud, north-east of Damascus; the Assyrian Assurbanipal, he adds, names both Yabrudu and Cubiti, that is Zobah, in his annals, and its kings are already spoken of in 1 Samuel 14:47. Hadadezer, he judges, had brought the whole country under a single sceptre, which is why his fall is so decisive. The occasion of the war — David smiting him "as he went to stablish his dominion by the river Euphrates" — Ellicott connects with the fuller account of the Syrian coalition in 1 Chronicles 19:16-19; and he parses "to stablish his dominion" as the Hebrew "to set up his hand," his power, where Samuel's slightly different word means rather "to recover" or renew his attack. Even "the river Euphrates," he notes, is the chronicler's explanatory expansion of Samuel's bare "the river."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the moral the bare geography leaves implicit. Such is the uncertainty of this world, he says, that men frequently lose their wealth and power at the very moment they think to confirm it: Hadadezer was smitten as he went to establish his dominion. The mightiest king of the north, marching to set up his hand at the great river, is broken on the way; and the reader is taught not to reckon on a greatness that God can shatter in the act of its own securing. The single grammatical-historical voice and the lone puritan preacher meet on the same ground — the one tracing the name and the map, the other the providence that overturns the ambitious in mid-stride.
4:
The spoils of the victory over Zobah, and David's striking treatment of the captured cavalry, draw out both the numbers and their meaning. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the count first. Chronicles reports "a thousand chariots, and seven thousand horsemen," where the Hebrew of Samuel reads "a thousand and seven hundred horsemen"; but since the territory of Zobah lay in the great plain of Aram, where a king would be strong in chariots and horses, Ellicott judges the chronicler's larger reading probably correct, comparing the parallel figure at 1 Chronicles 19:18. He explains the verb "houghed" as "hamstrung" — the cutting of the sinews of the hind legs to disable the animals — and notes that the same Hebrew term rendered "chariot horses" can also mean the chariot-soldiers. David, he observes, reserved a hundred chariots with their horses, probably for his own use, since horses were always a luxury in Israel, a point he ties to Isaiah's complaint that the land had become full of them in Isaiah 2:7, and to the law's express prohibition against a king multiplying horses in Deuteronomy 17:16 — the very law Solomon would later strain when he recruited his stud from Egypt.

Where Ellicott sees a prudent disabling of enemy cavalry, the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sees a confession of faith. David himself had written that "a horse is a vain thing for safety" in Ps. 33:17, and it seems, says Henry, that he believed what he said, for he houghed the chariot-horses rather than trust to them; being resolved, in the spirit of Ps. 20:7, not to boast in chariots and horses but in the name of the LORD his God, he would not use them. The act that looks at first like mere military calculation becomes, on Henry's reading, a deliberate renunciation: the king who could have fielded the finest cavalry of the north deliberately lames it, lest Israel's trust migrate from the living God to the arm of flesh.

The two schools do not contend; they illumine the same deed from two sides. Ellicott secures the text and the custom — the disabling of hostile chariotry, the sparing of a hundred for the royal service, the luxury horses always were in Israel; Henry secures the heart, the theology of a warrior-king who would rather cripple a captured strength than let it become a rival to his hope in God. Together they leave the verse as a portrait of victory held with an open hand, its spoils enjoyed only so far as they do not corrupt the trust that won them.
5:
When Damascus marches to the aid of the falling Hadadezer, the witnesses read the verse as both a point of philology and a parable of doomed alliances. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening literally as "And Aram of Damascus came" — the verb here masculine where Samuel has it feminine — and lingers over the city's name, "Darmeseq," a late form of the older "Dammeseq," which recurs in 2 Chronicles 28:5 and echoes across the languages: the Syriac "Darmesuq," the Arabic "Dimashqu," the cuneiform "Dimashqa." The clause "David slew of the Syrians two and twenty thousand men" he reads with care as "smote in Aram," the Hebrew preposition being partitive — not that David destroyed the whole nation, but that he struck down a great part of it. The grammar is small, but it keeps the slaughter within its true bounds: a crushing blow, not an annihilation.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds in the Syrians' errand the deeper lesson. The enemies of God's church, he observes, are often made to ruin themselves by helping one another: the Syrians of Damascus were smitten precisely because they came to succour Hadadezer. When hand thus joins in hand to oppose the people of God, they shall not only not go unpunished, but their very confederacy gathers them for judgment — heaped up, as the prophet says, like sheaves brought into the threshing-floor in Micah 4:11-12. The alliance that was meant to save Zobah only enlarges the ruin, drawing a second nation into the slaughter that had already claimed the first.

So the two voices, the grammarian and the preacher, converge on a single scene from opposite approaches. Ellicott fixes the reading — Aram of Damascus advancing, a partitive smiting of two and twenty thousand, the ancient name of the city preserved through a dozen tongues — while Henry draws the providence, that the coalition of the wicked is self-defeating, and that those who reinforce the enemies of God do but multiply the harvest of his judgment. The verse thus does double duty in the chapter's argument: it swells the tale of David's victories, and it exhibits the futility of every hand raised, in league with another, against the anointed king through whom God is establishing his kingdom.
6:
The verse marks the moment Damascus itself passes under David's hand, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works patiently through its diction. "Then David put garrisons in Syria of Damascus" — though he notes that the noun for "garrisons" has here fallen out of the Hebrew, probably by an oversight, and must be supplied from the parallel, where the word elsewhere means an outpost or garrison, and in 1 Kings 4:19 the prefects or governors set over a district. The versions grope after it variously — the Targum's "generals," the Syriac and Arabic "prefects" or "collectors," the Greek and Latin "garrison." "The Syrians became servants to David," he adds, matching Samuel's order "to David servants," and Hadadezer's realm is now a vassal kingdom, its master not dethroned but made tributary.

Then comes the refrain that the chapter will sound again: "and the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went." Ellicott hears in the Hebrew a shade the English loses — the word rendered "preserved" carries the sense "made victorious" — and notes that Chronicles uses a later construction than Samuel's, the same phrase "whithersoever he went" that stood in the promise of 1 Chronicles 17:8. He is careful, too, about the "shields of gold" of the next clause: the Hebrew word probably means armour or arms in general, a term that ranges elsewhere over spears, shields, and quivers, so that the golden trophies carried off may have been ornamented weapons or bucklers borne by Hadadezer's guard.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes the refrain and makes it the heart of the verse. Those who take God along with them wheresoever they go, he says, may expect to prosper and be preserved wheresoever they go; and it is twice written of David, here and again at the rout of Edom, that "the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went," for those are always under the eye of God who have God always in their eye. The conquest of Damascus is thus no bare feat of arms but a specimen of a life lived under providence: the victory is Ellicott's "made victorious," and the security is Henry's watchful God. The witnesses agree that the true garrison holding David's frontier is not the troops he stations in Syria but the unsleeping care of the LORD who goes with him.
7:
The golden shields of Hadadezer's servants are carried up to Jerusalem, and though the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott has here only the slightest of notes — remarking that the chronicler's phrase "on the servants of Hadarezer" answers to Samuel's "to," in the sense of arms "belonging to" those officers — the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds in the destination of the spoil one of the chapter's richest veins. The wealth of the sinner, he says, sometimes proves to have been laid up for the just: the shields of gold that had adorned the bodyguard of a heathen king are brought into the city of God, and what once served the pride of Damascus is turned to the enriching of the LORD's worship.

Henry raises the observation into a type. As the tabernacle in the wilderness had been built of the spoils the Israelites carried out of Egypt, so the temple that David's son would raise was to be enriched from the spoils of the Gentile nations — a happy presage, he says, of the interest the Gentiles should one day have in the gospel church. The golden trophies of a conquered enemy, devoted to God, quietly foreshadow the day when the treasures and the peoples of the nations shall be gathered in, no longer as plunder dragged behind a conqueror's chariot but as willing worshippers bringing their glory into the sanctuary. It begins, in miniature, with these bucklers of gold, laid up by David against the house he was himself forbidden to build.

So the verse, slight in the telling, is weighty in its direction. David does not hoard the spoil of Aram for his own magnificence; he carries it to Jerusalem and lays it by for the service of God, so that the very gold of the enemy becomes an ornament of praise. The witnesses leave the reader to feel the reversal: the strength and splendour of the nations, stripped from them in judgment, are received again in mercy as the furniture of worship — a small notice of golden shields that opens, under Henry's hand, upon the whole purpose of God to gather the Gentiles in. The next verse will carry the same lesson forward in the brass of the conquered cities, gathered for the same holy use.
8:
From the cities of the defeated Hadadezer David carries off a great weight of bronze, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott devotes his attention to the two obscure place-names and to the metal itself. "Tibhath and Chun," he says, are two otherwise unknown towns; where Chronicles has these, Samuel reads "Betah and Berothai." "Tebah" appears as an Aramean name already in Genesis 22:24, of which "Tibhath" is a feminine form and "Betah" probably a corruption; the versions, he shows, largely support a form like Tebah, while "Chun" is doubtless corrupt and the readings point rather to "Berothai." The textual thicket is dense, but Ellicott's point is steady: behind the variant spellings lie real Syrian cities, stripped of their bronze by the victorious king.

On the metal he is precise. The "much brass" of the older versions is better called copper, as in Job 28:2, or bronze, the alloy of copper and tin well known to the ancients; Samuel's phrase, "copper in abundance," preserves an older form of expression. And Ellicott marks a clause that Chronicles adds which the Hebrew of Samuel lacks — the note that it was with this bronze that Solomon afterward made the great furnishings of the temple, though the Greek of Samuel supplies the same. That addition is the whole significance of the verse: the raw spoil of a northern war is being traced forward to the sacred vessels of the house of God.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already gathered that meaning into his lesson on the chapter. The shields of gold and the brass alike, he says, were brought to Jerusalem and dedicated by David toward the building and enriching of the temple; and as the tabernacle had been made of the spoils of the Egyptians, so the temple would be built of the spoils of other Gentile nations. Here that pattern becomes concrete: the bronze of Tibhath and Chun, borne to Jerusalem, is the very stuff of which the brazen sea and the pillars and the vessels of Solomon's temple would be cast. The witnesses converge on a quiet marvel — that the plunder of Aram, gathered in the violence of conquest, is laid up by a warrior forbidden to build, and rises at last, transfigured, into the furniture of worship. What the sword took from the nations, the altar would give back to God.
9:
The tide of David's success now reaches an unconquered neighbour, who chooses friendship over war, and the witnesses take up both the king's name and the wisdom of his overture. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott settles the name: Chronicles' "Tou" answers to Samuel's "Toi," the Hebrew letters for w and y being easily confused in the manuscripts, and "Tou" is the right form, supported by the Greek and Latin. He records the versions' wanderings with a scholar's relish — the Syriac here reads, absurdly, "Phul king of the Antiochenes," the Arabic "Phawil king of Antioch," an apparent confusion with Pul the Assyrian named in 1 Chronicles 5:26 — and he notes that Professor Sayce believed he had read the very name, Tou, on the Hamath stones now in the British Museum. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds only the plain cross-reference, that this Tou is called Toi in the parallel at 2 Samuel 8:9.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the diplomacy into doctrine. It is our interest, he says, to make those our friends who have the presence of God with them: the king of Hamath, hearing of David's great success, sent to congratulate him and to court his favour with a noble present, and herein is a lesson for all. It is vain to contend with the Son of David; therefore "Kiss the Son, lest he be angry," and let the kings and judges of the earth, and the meanest people too, be thus wise and thus instructed. Yet Henry lifts the gift itself higher than gold: the presents we are to bring to the true King are not vessels of silver, as here — for those shall be welcome to him who have no such presents to bring — but our hearts and sincere affections, our whole selves offered as living sacrifices.

So the verse joins the historian's precision to the preacher's appeal. Ellicott and Clarke fix who Tou was and how his name travelled through the versions; Henry hears in his embassy the voice of prudence itself, a Gentile prince reading the signs of the times and seeking peace with the man God has so plainly blessed. The chapter that has been all conquest turns for a moment to homage freely offered, and the witnesses agree that such homage is the wise response of every ruler, and every soul, to the King before whom resistance is folly and submission is life.
10:
The embassy of the previous verse is now named and sent, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the details their due. "He sent Hadoram his son" — and this Hadoram, he observes, appears in Samuel as "Joram" (see the parallel at 2 Samuel 8:10). The forms "Hadoram" or "Adoram," meaning "Hadar is high," seem right; yet "Joram," that is Jehoram, "Jehovah is high," may be correct after all, for an inscription of Sargon shows that the God of Israel was not unknown even among the Hamathites — Sargon names one of their kings Iahu-bihdi, a name compounded with the sacred Name. The possibility is a striking one: a Syrian prince bearing, perhaps, a name that confesses the LORD, sent to salute the king through whom the LORD was conquering. "To congratulate" David, Ellicott adds, is literally to "bless" him, to pronounce him happy; and the notice that Tou "had war with" Hadadezer means, in the Hebrew, that Hadadezer had been "a man of wars," a standing foeman, of Tou. The catalogue of gifts — "all manner of vessels of gold and silver and brass" — is the chronicler's somewhat curtailed form of Samuel's fuller "vessels of silver, and vessels of gold, and vessels of brass."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, who reads verses nine and ten together, presses the meaning of the present. The king of Hamath courts David's favour with a noble gift because he perceives it vain to contend with one so manifestly owned by heaven; and the embassy of Hadoram is the visible act of that prudence, a rival delivered from the common enemy hastening to secure the friendship of the victor. Henry will draw out in the next verse what David did with such tribute; here it is enough that a Gentile king, unbidden and unbeaten, comes bearing treasure to the anointed of Israel.

The verse, then, sets a son of Hamath before the throne of David with gold in his hands and a blessing on his lips, and the witnesses find in the small scene a large hint. The grammatical-historical scholar uncovers, in the prince's very name, a possible whisper of the God of Israel reaching into the courts of Aram; the puritan preacher hears the homage of the nations beginning, as kings who cannot conquer choose instead to bless. The kingdom David is establishing draws tribute not only from the vanquished but from the wise, who come while they may.
11:
The gathered treasure of many wars is now devoted to God, and the witnesses read the verse as the spiritual summit of the chapter. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording and the list. "King David dedicated" the spoil, he notes — where the chronicler's verb, "he brought," avoids the tautology of Samuel's "dedicated"; and "these nations" means "the nations whom he had reduced." On the roll of them he corrects a reading: Chronicles' "from Edom" stands where Samuel has "from Aram," but the Greek, Syriac, and Arabic all read "Edom" here, which Ellicott judges correct, since Edom and Moab were conterminous and the reference means to sweep in all the nations David conquered and despoiled. "And from Amalek" may point back to the recovery of the spoil at Ziklag in 1 Samuel 30:16, or more probably to some campaign left unrecorded; and Chronicles omits Samuel's added clause about the spoil of Hadadezer, as already implied in the bronze of vv.7-8.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the verse a rule of stewardship. What God blesses us with, he says, we must honour him with: the presents of David's friends, as here from Tou, and the spoils of his enemies alike, the king dedicated unto the LORD — that is, he laid them up toward the building and enriching of the temple. That, Henry urges, is most truly and most comfortably our own which we have consecrated to God and use for his glory; let our merchandise and our hire be "holiness to the LORD," as the prophet says in Isaiah 23:18. The treasure passes out of David's hands into God's, and in that surrender becomes, for the first time, securely his own.

So the two schools frame the same act. Ellicott catalogues the nations — Edom, Moab, Ammon, the Philistines, Amalek — and secures the text that names them; Henry catalogues the heart, and secures the motive that sanctifies the spoil. Together they show a conqueror who does not build himself a treasury but a sanctuary, dedicating the wealth of the subdued peoples to the God who gave the victories. The verse gathers all the chapter's scattered triumphs into one offering, and in doing so points past David to the Son of David, whose kingdom is enriched not with the plunder of the nations but with the nations themselves, brought as an offering to God.
12:
The scene shifts abruptly to the south and to a subordinate's victory, and the witnesses must reconcile the several accounts of who won it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke notes that the same triumph is credited to David himself in the parallel at 2 Samuel 8:13; the reconciliation, he says, is simple and just — David sent Abishai against the Edomites, and Abishai defeated them, so that the victory is rightly ascribed to the king as commander-in-chief, on the old maxim "qui facit per alterum, facit per se," he who acts through another acts himself. The one who commands the campaign owns its success, whichever captain's hand strikes the blow.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays the three passages side by side. Chronicles reads plainly that "Abishai the son of Zeruiah smote of the Edomites in the Valley of Salt eighteen thousand"; Samuel has something quite different — "David made him a name when he returned from smiting Aram" — where "Aram" should be read "Edom," as the Greek, Syriac, and Arabic have it, the two words differing by a single easily-confused letter. From a comparison of the accounts, including the title of Psalm 60, Ellicott reconstructs the history: Edom took advantage of David's absence in the north to invade Judah, whereupon the king detached a column of his forces and sent it south under Joab and Abishai to repulse the new enemy. The "Valley of Salt," he adds, is the same battlefield where a later king of Judah would again smite Edom, as recorded in 2 Kings 14:7.

The witnesses do not divide; they complete one another. Clarke secures the principle by which David and Abishai are both truly credited, and Ellicott recovers the campaign behind the compressed notice — a diversionary invasion from the south, met and crushed while the main army was busy with Aram in the north. The verse, brief and easily overlooked, thus fills out the picture of a kingdom pressed on every side and victorious on every side: north against Zobah and Damascus, south against Edom in the Valley of Salt, the king's authority reaching wherever his captains march. It is one more stone in the chapter's steady building of David's dominion, and one more instance of the LORD who, as the refrain insists, preserved David whithersoever he went.
13:
Edom is garrisoned and subdued, and the chapter's tale of conquest closes where it began, on the note of divine preservation. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains that David "put garrisons," or governors, in Edom — the same word rendered "garrisons" at the taking of Damascus — and that Samuel's fuller phrase, "in all Edom he set garrisons," marks the complete subjugation of the country. Then he gathers the whole campaign into a single survey. "Thus the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went": the king had been victorious on every side, north against Zobah and the Syrians, and south and east and west against Moab, Ammon, the Philistines, Amalek, and Edom. The six peoples he reduced, Ellicott points out, were the very nations that had been the foes of his ill-fated predecessor Saul, whose wars against them are listed in 1 Samuel 14:47-48 — but where Saul had only harassed them, David subdues them, and the contrast between the two reigns is written into the map.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry fastens, as at the conquest of Damascus, on the refrain that frames the section. It was said before, at v.6, and here it is deliberately repeated, that "the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went." Those who take God along with them wheresoever they go, Henry teaches, may expect to prosper and be preserved wheresoever they go; and this double seal upon David's expeditions is set down that the reader may see the true cause of his unbroken success. It was not the strength of his garrisons nor the number of his captains, but the presence of God, that carried him safe through every quarter of the compass.

The witnesses converge on the theology the geography serves. Ellicott shows the extent of the victory — a ring of subdued enemies where Saul had known only trouble; Henry shows its secret — a God who keeps the man who keeps his eye on God. The verse thus rounds off the record of David's wars: the borders are secure, the old enemies are servants, the empire is established, and every stroke of it is credited not to the conqueror's arm but to the LORD who went with him. What began as a chapter of battles ends as a testimony to providence, and prepares the turn, in the verse to come, from the winning of a kingdom to the just ruling of it.
14:
The wars won, the chapter turns to the peace they secured, and the witnesses read this summary of David's reign as its moral crown. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, though brief, is exact. "David executed judgment and justice" he renders as "was doing" them — not a single act but a permanent state of things, the settled character of the reign. "Judgment and justice" he parses as "right and justice," the former the quality and the latter the conduct that embodies it, so that the phrase names both the inward rectitude and its outward practice; and the little word "among all his people" he notes means "for" or "unto" them, the king's justice reaching to the benefit of every subject. In four short glosses he draws the portrait of a government that is not occasional but constant, not partial but universal, and not merely upright in the abstract but active in the doing of right to all.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry expands that portrait into a doctrine of power. God gives men authority, he says, not that they may look great with it, but that they may do good with it; and when David reigned over all Israel he executed judgment and justice among all his people, and so answered the very end of his elevation. He was not so intent on his conquests abroad as to neglect the administration of justice at home — a rare balance in a warrior-king, and the mark of one who ruled for God and not for himself. Herein, Henry says, David served the purposes of the God who sits on the throne judging right, and became an eminent type of the Messiah, the sceptre of whose kingdom is a right sceptre.

So the historian and the preacher meet on the chapter's final and highest note. Ellicott's grammar makes the justice permanent and comprehensive; Henry's theology makes it the true purpose of the power the earlier verses have been heaping up. All the victories were for this — that a king might sit over all Israel and do right to all his people. And in that just and universal reign the witnesses see the shadow of a greater King, of whom the psalmist sings in Psalms 45:6, "a sceptre of righteousness is the sceptre of thy kingdom." The dominion David wins by the sword he holds by equity, and in holding it so he images the Son of David, whose everlasting throne is established in righteousness.
15:
With the empire won and justice established, the chapter closes with the roll of David's chief officers — the machinery of the well-ordered kingdom the previous verse has praised — and the witnesses gloss the first two posts. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke names their functions plainly: Joab, set "over the host," was general-in-chief, the commander of all David's armies; and Jehoshaphat the son of Ahilud, the "recorder," was the king's remembrancer, or historiographer royal — the officer charged to keep the memorials of the reign and to bring matters of moment to the king's notice.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dwells on that second title. "Recorder," he says, is literally "Remembrancer"; the Greek and the Latin render the word as one set "over," or the "writer of," the memoranda of the kingdom, while the Syriac and Arabic of the parallel make him rather a "leader" or "director." The office was no mere clerkship: to keep the royal records, to preserve the acts of state, and to stand as the king's memory and mouthpiece was a place of real weight, and it recurs among the great officers of Judah down the generations, as in the reforms of a much later reign recorded in 2 Chronicles 34:8. Ellicott's care over the word guards against reading the ancient court by modern categories; the remembrancer was closer to a chancellor than to a scribe.

The witnesses, though occupied with a mere list, let its significance show. A kingdom is not held by conquest alone but by administration, and the historian who has just told of David's justice now names the men through whom that justice was done — a soldier to guard the realm and a remembrancer to record and order its affairs. Clarke and Ellicott agree in substance, differing only in emphasis: the one fixes the plain duties of the two officers, the other the exact force of the ancient titles. Between them they open the door on David's government, and prepare the fuller catalogue of priests, secretary, and bodyguard that the closing verses will complete. The kingdom established by the sword is now seen to be sustained by order, each office in its place under the king who does right to all his people.
16:
The list moves to the sacred and secretarial offices, and here the witnesses meet a genuine tangle of names. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the verse simply: Zadok and Abimelech were both high priests — the one ministering at Gibeon, where the tabernacle then stood, the other at Jerusalem before the ark, as the arrangement of 1 Chronicles 16:39 had shown — while Shavsha the scribe is the officer called Seraiah in the parallel at 2 Samuel 8:17.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott probes the crux more deeply. Zadok, he notes, was of the line of Eleazar, traced in 1 Chronicles 6:4-8; but "Abimelech the son of Abiathar" should be read "Ahimelech the son of Abiathar," as Samuel has it. And here lies the difficulty: elsewhere it is Zadok and Abiathar who figure as the priests of David's reign, and since that Abiathar was himself the son of the Ahimelech slain at Nob by Saul's order in 1 Samuel 22:20, some have proposed to invert the names here and read "Abiathar the son of Ahimelech." Ellicott declines the emendation as far from certain. An "Ahimelech son of Abiathar" who was priest in David's time is named three times over in 1 Chronicles 24:3 and following, and this man may well have been acting as deputy for his father when the brief list was drawn up; in the absence of details, Ellicott judges, it would be arbitrary to alter the text of four separate passages to force a harmony. He notes, finally, that "Shavsha" appears elsewhere as "Sheva," and that "Seraiah" seems to have been the secretary's original name.

So the witnesses divide, mildly, over the priest's name. Clarke takes the text as it stands and is content that two high priests served the two sanctuaries; Ellicott weighs the proposed correction and, though he sees its force, refuses to impose it on so many passages. The disagreement is not over doctrine but over discipline of text — whether to smooth an apparent confusion by emendation or to let the received reading stand and seek a historical explanation. The verse, for all its bare cataloguing, thus opens a small window on the labour of transmission, and reminds the reader that even the roll of David's officers has come down through the hands of copyists, its very names a matter for reverent care.
17:
The roll ends with the royal bodyguard and the princes of the blood, and the witnesses draw out both the identity of the guard and a delicate change the chronicler has made. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains that Benaiah the son of Jehoiada was set "over the Cherethites and the Pelethites," the royal body-guard, for which office Oriental kings have always employed foreign mercenaries — Josephus plainly calls them the king's guard. The names are tribal in form: the Cherethites recur in connection with the Philistines in Ezekiel 25:16 and Zephaniah 2:5, and the name "Pelethites" resembles "Philistines," so that David's guard was most likely recruited from two Philistine tribes, as their appearance alongside a corps of Gittites in 2 Samuel 15:18 would confirm.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds the Targum's expansive gloss, which makes Benaiah president over the great and the small Sanhedrin and the consulter of Urim and Thummim, at whose command the archers and slingers went out to battle; and he notes that "the sons of David were the highest in authority," chief about the king. It is that last phrase over which Ellicott lingers longest. Chronicles reads that the sons of David were "the first at the king's hand"; but Samuel had said they were "chief rulers" — in the Hebrew, the ordinary word for "priests," which had just occurred in that plain sense in the verse before. The chronicler, writing from the standpoint of a later age when the priesthood was strictly Levitical, has substituted a phrase that would not offend contemporary feeling, lest David's sons seem to usurp a sacred office — much as, in 1 Kings 4:5, the same word denotes a secular minister of state.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same grammatical-historical school, had marked this very paraphrase as one of the chronicler's characteristic explanatory touches, set beside his rendering of the "bridle of the mother" in the first verse. The witnesses converge on a picture of the fully ordered court: a trusted captain commands the foreign guard, the king's own sons stand first at his hand, and the chronicler, in the very act of listing them, betrays his care to render an old record intelligible and inoffensive to his own day. Ellicott and Keil recover the history behind the softened phrase, Clarke preserves the tradition that magnified Benaiah's charge, and all leave the reign of David complete — won by conquest, crowned with justice, and served by an ordered household of soldiers, priests, and princes under the king whom the LORD preserved whithersoever he went.
